1. Home
  2. Evaluating AI for legal reasoning and support - AdSoLve Stakeholder Workshop

Event Overview: Evaluating AI for Legal Reasoning and Support - AdSoLve Stakeholder Workshop

This latest edition of the AdSoLve Legal AI workshop addressed the trends of evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) in legal settings, from benchmarks to practical solutions. It covered talks from experts in academia, law firms, real-world system demos, and representatives from non-profit organisations offering legal advice. Throughout the day, participants discussed how technical evaluation can better reflect professional workflows, stakeholder needs, and context-specific governance.

Navigating the AI Frontier: Insights from the AdSoLVE Legal AI Workshop

Professor Maria Liakata opened the workshop by outlining one of AdSoLVE’s key goals: to evaluate and mitigate the socio-technical limitations of large language models, particularly in high-stakes settings such as healthcare and law. Her talk framed evaluation as essential to responsible deployment, especially where LLM outputs may affect evidence use, legal reasoning, privacy, bias, trust and downstream decisions. Rather than relying on saturated benchmarks, generic metrics or prompt-sensitive LLM-as-judge methods, she argued for multi-faceted evaluation able to assess technical quality of outputs from multiple perspectives and connect it to context-specific needs and priorities. She positioned transparent reference-free evaluation, stakeholder co-creation, temporal and situational reasoning, multimodal data handling, privacy preservation and practical guardrails as core to aligning AI systems with professional practice and public accountability.

The subsequent talks developed this agenda across complementary strands. Guneet Kohli showed how the quality of generated outputs (e.g. answers to questions) can be assessed by evaluating the reasoning that produced them (not just the final answer) by creating graph-based representations of such reasoning to identify unsupported, contradictory or disconnected steps. This transparent, reference-free method can be used for any kind of output and model. Mahmud Akhter introduced the RECV benchmark for evidence-based claim verification, highlighting how abductive reasoning becomes harder as real-world noise increases. Xingwei Tan discussed training models on symbolic deductive, inductive and abductive tasks to encourage more structured out-of-domain reasoning and reduce ungrounded overthinking. Mahmud then examined persona prompting through task performance, token saliency and layer-wise mechanism analysis, asking when personas help or hinder reasoning. Sebastian presented the AdSoLve evaluation platform and showed how it can be used to evaluate court case summaries, illustrating the range of aspects and metrics contributing to multi-faceted evaluation.

The Business of Legal AI: Return on Investment (ROI) and the Verification Burden

In the first invited talk, Kirsten Maslen (Thomson Reuters) reframed legal AI evaluation around deployment, trust and commercial value and not model capability alone. Her central warning was that adoption is moving faster than governance, measurement and evaluation, therefore leaving firms exposed if they are unable to explain where AI adds value and how risks are supervised.

Maslen argued that ROI depends on the verification burden: time savings only count if outputs can be checked cost-effectively, especially where the cost of being wrong is high. She highlighted three value dimensions: efficiency, greater matter throughput and capability expansion, including AI-enabled services.

For academics and legal stakeholders, the key message was that legal AI should be assessed as part of professional service delivery, with evaluation tied to workflow fit, auditability, client value and responsible use.

Innovating with Caution: Incorporated Council of Law Reporting's (ICLR) AI Strategy

In the second invited talk, Paul Magrath showed how ICLR is deploying AI while protecting its core standard: expert, accurate law reporting. He presented one of their systems, Case Genie, which addresses legal research for “unknown unknowns” (cases that are not indexed or reported but would still need to be researched) by analysing uploaded text, extracting cited authorities, and recommending similar or citation-linked cases. As the technology behind Case Genie is based on retrieval, recommendation or information extraction, there is little risk for fabricated content. He also presented a system for summarisation based on conservative use of generative-AI: GPT-4 provides 100-word summaries and issue lists for unreported judgments, developed with Jurisage. These short factual outputs help users decide what to read, but are not headnotes, court-ready citations, or substitutes for professional legal judgment. ICLR evaluates them through reporter review, mechanical checks, and editorial correction.

AI and the Justice Gap: How Public Advice Seekers Use Chatbots

In our third invited talk, Ellen Lefley (JUSTICE UK) shifted the focus on legal AI from professional tools to non-lawyers using free, general-purpose chatbots for legal matters. Drawing on JUSTICE’s collaboration with the Administrative Fairness Lab, she reported a survey of 3,287 people: 1,428 had faced a legal dispute in the previous two years, and 233 of them (16.3%) had used an AI chatbot for help, information or advice.

The findings are policy-relevant. AI users are tentatively skewed towards (younger, male, full-time employed and more likely to be Black), though Lefley cautioned against over-reading small cell counts. Only 6% used AI alone; most combined it with Google or other sources. Shared chats covered consumer, housing, tenancy and employment disputes, but also needs beyond legal information: drafting support, procedural reassurance and scripts for difficult conversations. The talk highlighted an access-to-justice challenge: protecting advice seekers without blocking scarce support.

Automating Small Claims: Garfield AI's Bounded Architecture

In the fourth invited talk, Daniel Long presented Garfield AI, the UK's first SRA-regulated AI law firm for automating small-claims civil litigation. The system uses a hybrid architecture: deterministic procedural logic manages a claim “state tree,” while LLMs extract and classify information from contracts, invoices and correspondence under schema validation. The demo showed Garfield providing rationales, proposing next procedural steps, and generating documents from letters. Crucially, Long stressed that Garfield does not give merits advice or make complex legal judgments.

For academics and stakeholders, the talk surfaces research needs around source-grounded extraction, knowledge graphs for heterogeneous evidence, extensions to cases that require more complex reasoning, and severity-aware evaluation.

The “Plumbing” of Legal AI: Avoiding Captive-Bred Evaluation

In his invited talk, Sam Grange (iManage) argued that legal AI evaluation is structurally over-focused on the visible LLM generation layer, while the “plumbing” that makes systems work in practice—query reformulation, preprocessing, retrieval, chunking, metadata, prompts, safety, tone and governance—remains under-evaluated. Citing AdSoLve’s review of 140 papers on legal applications of LLMs, he noted that 30.7% use multiple-choice accuracy, a metric, he agreed with our review, which is poorly matched to real legal work. His DSPy anecdote showed the risk: optimisation against output accuracy stripped out carefully researched guidance on tone, safety and alignment because the metric could not reward it. Using a “captive breeding” analogy, Grange warned that narrow fitness functions can produce systems that look impressive but fail in the wild. He urged diagnostic, workflow-level evaluation sensors that reveal where legal AI systems actually break.

Personas, Plumbing, and the Adoption Gap

In the day’s final invited talk, Illich Real (Rubiklab) argued that legal AI’s adoption gap is not just about model capability: users often struggle to value the invisible “plumbing” behind useful systems, including data preparation, tagging and workflow design. In his board-evaluation demo, complex backend analysis was hidden behind a simple persona-driven interface offering analytical, opportunity-seeking, operational and risk-sensitive viewpoints.

For academics and stakeholders, the talk reframes legal AI evaluation around non-binary professional needs, organisational knowledge, usability and counterpoints to human cognitive bias in remote or distributed work.

Procurement, Governance, and the Agentic Frontier

In the panel session, John Keers (Ulster University), Kirsten Maslen (Thomson Reuters), Sam Grange (iManage) and Richard Nicholas (Browne Jacobson) examined how legal AI procurement is changing as firms move from generative tools toward more agentic workflows.

The Limits of Guidance: The panel treated the Law Society’s recent IT procurement guidance as useful, especially for firms without large innovation teams, but cautioned that professional guidance is struggling to keep pace with rapidly evolving autonomous and agentic systems.

Supporting Smaller Firms: Smaller firms were encouraged to draw on professional bodies, legal-tech communities, peer-shared vendor “bake-offs,” and open-source schemas such as NOSLegal to structure knowledge bases and improve AI readiness.

Rethinking Vendor Evaluation: Successful pilots require lawyer-led testing within specific practice areas, clear success metrics, and willingness to examine failure data, not just vendor success stories. Richard Nicholas also warned that firms should scrutinise licence-back clauses covering inputs and outputs, as some initial contract terms were too onerous for legal practice.

The Agentic Workflow Dilemma: The panel was cautiously excited but concerned about error propagation. Firms should not rely on agents to govern themselves; they need human review points, version control, audit trails, permissions, and zero-trust infrastructure around agentic systems.

The core message was that legal AI procurement is no longer simple software adoption: it is an evolving socio-technical governance challenge.

Evaluating Legal AI: Requirements Across Risk Contexts

The day ended with breakout sessions that tested how stakeholder requirements can be translated into socio-technical evaluation metrics for contract-analysis AI. Participants supported evaluating omission, hallucination, failure handling, structural validity, evidence grounding and consistency, but stressed that these concepts need sharper legal definitions. A central issue was that traceability is not enough: an output may cite source material yet still draw an invalid legal inference. Risk context shaped what counted as proportionate evaluation. For routine, human-reviewed tenancy-deposit claims involving a single standard contract, RAG citation and cross-document consistency could be unnecessary overhead. In high-stakes arbitration across multiple contracts, the same checks become central, alongside stricter thresholds, escalation routes and scrutiny of legal interpretation. The main takeaway is that automated metrics are partial proxies: evaluation platforms should provide transparent, context-sensitive evidence for justified human trust, allowing humans to make appropriate judgements.

Looking Ahead: Co-Creating Context-Aware Metrics

The closing discussion reinforced that trustworthy legal AI evaluation is an ongoing collaborative process, not a static technical exercise. Moderators and participants highlighted the need to co-create and constructively critique metrics so they genuinely reflect stakeholder requirements rather than simply measure what is easiest to quantify. Breakout discussions showed that recurring output-generation issues such as hallucination, grounding, omission and consistency require sharper legal definitions, and that their importance changes with context. An evaluation step that may be unnecessary overhead for a low-stakes, human-reviewed task can become central in high-stakes legal scenarios. The workshop therefore concluded with a clear direction for AdSoLVE: to develop evaluation frameworks that are transparent, context-sensitive and grounded in professional practice, providing the necessary evidence for human experts to make informed judgements more efficiently.
